metadata.title: Make It Live: Website + Tool Demo Readiness
metadata.playlist: Make It Count — The Complete Brand System
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Capstone — Complete Brand System (200 pts, due 2026-08-14)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 6. Two things can sink you on presentation day before you say a word: a dead URL and a demo that will not run. Everything else in the capstone can be great — strategy, identity, stories, deck — and none of it matters if the grader clicks your website link and gets a 404, or your tool hangs on the second feature. This episode is about pre-flight: what live really means, what a working demo actually requires, what a backup looks like, and how to test from a device that is not yours. Let's go.

props.segment: Make It Live: Website + Tool Demo Readiness
props.command: Check my URLs and demo plan. Flag any localhost, missing backup, or unreachable link.
props.runningText: a dead URL and a demo that won't run — two things that sink a perfect capstone…
output: Live means live — what the test actually looks like
Backup plan + phone test — the pre-flight protocol

beats.act: live website — what live means live
beats.narration_text: Live means live. Not almost live. Not 'it works on my machine.' The definition: a URL that resolves in a browser on a device you do not own, over a network you do not control, without any setup from you. If you are running on localhost, you are not live. If your site is behind a password nobody else has, you are not live. If it only works on your university Wi-Fi, you are not live. The acceptable platforms for a live capstone site are Vercel, Framer, Wix, Squarespace, Webflow, GitHub Pages, or any other service that hosts a static or server-rendered site on a public URL. The site must load all pages the grader is expected to visit — not just the home page. Every link in the navigation must work. Every image must load. The deployment must be the current version, not the version from three weeks ago. The test is not 'I deployed it' — the test is 'I opened it on a phone I borrowed from someone who has never touched this project and every page loaded correctly.'

props.artifactTitle: Live means live — the real test
props.artifactHeading: Not 'it works on my machine' — a stranger's device, unknown network
artifactLines: Localhost = not live. Password-gated = not live. University-Wi-Fi-only = not live.
Acceptable: Vercel · Framer · Wix · Squarespace · Webflow · GitHub Pages.
Every nav link works. Every image loads. Current deployment, not 3-week-old version.
The test: open on a borrowed phone by someone who has never touched this project.
All pages the grader will visit must load — not just the home page.
props.sparkLine: If it needs your setup to work, it isn't live.

beats.act: working tool demo — link, features, methodology
beats.narration_text: The working tool demo. The grader needs to see the tool running — not a description of it, not a screenshot, not a mockup. A live link or credentials that anyone can use. Before presentation day, you need two or three key features identified and rehearsed. Not every feature — two or three that demonstrate the tool's core value clearly and in under three minutes. The methodology is the story behind those features: why this tool exists, what problem it solves, how the AI component works, and what makes your approach different from a generic solution. That methodology is what turns a demo into a presentation — without it, you are just showing software. Know the features cold. Know the order. Know how to recover if one fails. And have a fallback ready — which brings us to the next beat.

props.artifactTitle: Working tool demo — what you need
props.artifactHeading: Live link · 2–3 rehearsed features · methodology stated
artifactLines: Live link or credentials anyone can use — no 'let me set it up first.'
2–3 key features: demonstrate core value in under 3 minutes.
Methodology: why it exists · what problem it solves · how the AI component works.
Know the features cold — know the order — know how to recover if one fails.
Methodology turns a demo into a presentation. Without it, it's just software.
props.sparkLine: Demo = live features + the methodology that makes them matter.

beats.act: the backup — recorded fallback and money-stop discipline
beats.narration_text: The backup. Every live demo that has ever failed failed because someone assumed it would work. The backup is not optional — it is the discipline that separates professionals from students. A recorded fallback is a screen recording of the demo running correctly, captured before presentation day, with the same features in the same order. It is not glamorous. It is insurance. If the live demo fails — network drops, API rate-limits, credentials expire — you switch to the recording without apologizing and without breaking stride. The second piece of backup discipline: the money stop. If your tool makes any API call that costs money — an LLM generation, an image request, a cloud function — you need a hard stop in your rehearsal flow. Know exactly which step spends money. Know the cost per call. Have a pre-generated example ready so the live demo does not accidentally spend fifty dollars in front of the grader. The grader is not paying for your demo. The money stop is a professional habit you want to have before you are doing this in front of a client.

props.artifactTitle: The backup — recorded fallback + money stop
props.artifactHeading: Every live demo that failed assumed it would work
artifactLines: Recorded fallback: screen-record the demo running correctly, before presentation day.
Same features, same order — switch to it without apologizing if live fails.
Money stop: know which step spends money. Have a pre-generated example ready.
The grader is not paying for your demo — this is a professional habit.
No backup plan = a single point of failure in a 200-point presentation.
props.sparkLine: Backup is not optional. It's the discipline that separates pros from students.

beats.narration_text: The test protocol. Before presentation day, you run one test that tells you whether everything is actually live. Open your website on someone else's phone — not your laptop, not a browser where you are logged in, not a device that has ever touched your project. Browse every page the grader will see. Click every link in the navigation. Check that images load. Then open your tool demo on the same phone. Run through the two or three features you are presenting. Check that credentials work. Check that the backup recording plays. If anything fails on that phone, that failure is your only job tonight. Not the deck. Not the stories. Whatever broke on the phone. The phone test is binary: pass or fix. There is no partial credit for 'it almost worked.' Run the test at least two days before presentation day — not the night before, because some failures take time to resolve. A DNS propagation issue can take twenty-four hours. A deployment that needs an environment variable can take a support ticket. Two days is the minimum buffer.

props.artifactHeading: Binary: pass or fix. No partial credit for 'almost worked.'
artifactLines: Device: someone else's phone — never a browser where you're logged in.
Check: every page · every nav link · every image · tool credentials · backup recording.
If anything fails: that failure is your only job tonight.
Run the test at least 2 days before presentation — DNS/deploy fixes take time.
Pass = done. Fail = tonight's only job.
props.sparkLine: The phone test finds failures while there's still time to fix them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Make It Live. Live means live: a URL that resolves on a device you do not own, over a network you do not control. Localhost is not live. Password-gated is not live. A working demo requires a live link or credentials, two or three rehearsed features, and the methodology that makes those features meaningful. The backup is not optional — record the demo running correctly before presentation day. The money stop is a professional habit: know which step spends money and have a pre-generated example ready. The phone test is binary: pass or fix. Run it at least two days before presentation day. Episode 3 is the assembly and coherence audit.

props.artifactTitle: Make It Live: Website + Tool Demo Readiness
artifactLines: Live = resolves on a stranger's device, unknown network. Localhost is not live.
Working demo: live link · 2–3 rehearsed features · methodology stated.
Backup: recorded fallback in the same order. Switch without apologizing.
Money stop: know which step costs money — pre-generate before presenting.
Phone test: run 2+ days before presentation. Pass or fix, nothing in between.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run the pre-flight checklist now — before you touch the deck. Paste this prompt into Claude with your actual values filled in. The prompt reads: I am doing a pre-flight check for my INFO 7375 capstone presentation. I have a website and a tool demo that I need to verify are actually live and ready. Here is my information. Website URL: [paste the full URL]. Platform it is hosted on: [Vercel / Framer / Wix / Squarespace / other]. Last time I deployed an update: [date]. Pages the grader will visit: [list them]. Tool demo URL or access method: [paste URL or describe how the grader accesses it]. The two or three features I plan to demonstrate: [list them in order]. Whether any of those features make a paid API call: [yes or no — if yes, describe which step and what the cost is]. Whether I have a recorded fallback: [yes or no]. Whether I have tested from a device I do not own: [yes or no — if yes, describe the result; if no, say so]. Based on this, flag any pattern that looks like localhost, a missing backup plan, a potential money-stop failure, or an access problem a stranger would hit. Tell me the one change I must make before presentation day. After you get the response, make that one change — and then run the phone test.

props.segment: Make It Live: Website + Tool Demo Readiness
props.command: Website URL + platform + last deploy date · demo URL + 2–3 features · paid API calls? (yes/no) · recorded fallback? (yes/no) · tested on unknown device? (yes/no). Flag: localhost · missing backup · money-stop risk · access failure. One change I must make.
props.runningText: make that one change — then run the phone test…

beats.narration_text: Make It Live: Website and Tool Demo Readiness. Liam, in for Bear.
